utilityName: City of Round Rock Backflow Prevention
testingFrequency: Annual for high-hazard devices; every 7 years for low-hazard devices
dueBasis: Round Rock sends notices through BSI Online about 30 days before the testing due date. High-hazard devices require annual testing and low-hazard residential irrigation devices require testing every 7 years.
coveredPropertyTypes: Non-residential high-hazard properties such as restaurants, hospitals, and industries
Residential irrigation systems treated as low hazard
officialListLabel: Open the Round Rock registered tester list
penalties: If the owner misses testing, the City may test the device and add a $75 fee plus the actual test cost to the water bill. Failed devices get a 30-day repair and retest window.
sourceExcerpt: Round Rock requires annual testing for high-hazard devices, 7-year testing for low-hazard devices, and uses BSI Online for report submission and notice handling.
verdictSummary: Round Rock splits testing cadence by hazard class: annual for high-hazard devices and every 7 years for low-hazard residential devices.
whoIsAffected: Commercial high-hazard properties, residential pools with auto-fill, and residential irrigation systems covered by the City's backflow program.
residentialNotes: Round Rock residential users need to pay attention to the low-hazard seven-year rule and the places where that lower cadence no longer applies.
Pools with auto-fill and residential irrigation systems are common local triggers.
commercialNotes: Commercial and other high-hazard properties stay on the annual side of the program.
Missed testing can turn into City-arranged testing and added billing exposure.

annualTesting.summary: Round Rock annual testing is mainly the high-hazard side of the program, while some low-hazard residential irrigation devices move to a seven-year cycle.
highlights: Low-hazard residential irrigation devices are every seven years.
BSI Online controls notice and reporting workflow.
workflowSteps: Identify whether the device is in the high-hazard or low-hazard class.
Use the BSI notice and schedule the test with a registered BPAT.
Submit through BSI Online and repair within 30 days if the device fails.

irrigation.summary: Round Rock is one of the best pilot cities for irrigation because it explicitly distinguishes low-hazard residential irrigation from high-hazard annual cases.
highlights: Residential irrigation often falls into the low-hazard seven-year lane.
That does not mean every irrigation site is exempt from frequent testing.
Pools with auto-fill and other added hazards can move the property into a stricter category.
workflowSteps: Confirm whether the irrigation device is low hazard or tied to a higher-risk property condition.
Use a registered BPAT and BSI Online for submission.
If the device fails, repair and retest within 30 days.
Use the city notice and BSI Online confirmation number to schedule the test.
Hire a registered BPAT or make sure the licensed tester registers with the City before testing.
Have the tester submit the report through BSI Online.
If the device fails, repair and retest within 30 days.
failureHighlights: A failed device triggers a 30-day repair and retest clock.
Reports can be rejected if license or gauge information is not current with the City.
If the City has to step in for missed testing, the cost can be added to the owner's bill.

costBand.testingRange: Private quotes vary; if the City has to arrange a missed test it can add a $75 fee plus the actual test cost.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest cost depends on the assembly and contractor, and can be billed back if the City had to intervene.
costBand.pricingNotes: Use private quotes for market pricing, but do not ignore the City fee exposure in missed-test situations.
